content:
Does anyone know, if it is possible to use GDB&QEMU to debug the rust code in the kernel?

I set GDB up with QEMU as remote target. When I try to set breakpoints in Rust functions, they are ignored. For C functions it works fine.

Is there something special to be done for it to work?

It could be, but some generic functions and inline functions will not generate till you call or implement it.

For example:

image.png
@Oliver Mangold did you rule out mangling?

Gdb should be able to handle mangling. You do have to set a breakpoint on my_crate::my_function (or my_crate::some_module::my_function if your function is inside mod some_module {}) rather than just my_function though. b path/to/my/file.rs:1234 should also work for setting a breakpoint on a specific line.
Also unrelated to breakpoints, but you may want to use the rust-gdb wrapper that ships with rustc. This adds a bunch of pretty printers for rust types like slices.

Daniel Almeida schrieb:

Oliver Mangold did you rule out mangling?

I was wondering at first, too, if I need to use the mangled or unmangled names, but as @bjorn3 says, gdb seems to understand the names in rust syntax. But as said, it doesn't break when I set a breakpoint like this, even though it says it found the symbol.

What I didn't try, yet, was breaking on file:lineno instead. I will do that tomorrow.

bjorn3 schrieb:

Also unrelated to breakpoints, but you may want to use the rust-gdb wrapper that ships with rustc. This adds a bunch of pretty printers for rust types like slices.

Ok, I have to try this one.

What I actually could use, though, is a memory debugger. I'm hunting a quite nasty memory corruption. Kasan is quiet, so the best option should be a watchpoint in GDB to see who is overwriting my memory. Or is there another option?
qemu + gdb is working fine for me, memory watch points and all. gdb 15.2 and qemu 9.1.1

Actually I have a problem when trying to debug modules. There is some kind of problems with the python scripts that are supposed to resolve the symbols when you load a module. But it works fine if I make the module built-in.

Andreas Hindborg schrieb:

Actually I have a problem when trying to debug modules. There is some kind of problems with the python scripts that are supposed to resolve the symbols when you load a module. But it works fine if I make the module built-in.

I noticed that, too. Have to familiarize myself more with the debugging infrastructure eventually.

Okay, I managed to get debugging of built-in rust code working by disabling CONFIG_RANDOMIZE_BASE.

Unfortunately even when I hit a breakpoint in built-in code that is called from a module I cannot step out of the function into the module code.
You could build everything into the kernel image
You should also add nokaslr kernel command line option. Not sure how it works, maybe it just disables RANDOMIZE_BASE when enabled.

Andreas Hindborg schrieb:

You could build everything into the kernel image

Yes. It seems, though, I need to first stop the module from crashing during load. For some reason GDB doesn't accept breakpoints I set before the kernel is started. So when I have the chance to set a breakpoint it is already too late..
For future reference: debugging kernel modules works, when I run lx-symbols after loading the module.
